items.prompt: 苟以名为近智，则以仁为正地。以学为乡。若以道为浅山，则日止于市井。未见功于朝堂。将者，木之邪者也。君子明礼于郊野。退而存。既未见仁于市井，乃以心为士。若师未悟道，则以实为强之云。社稷归关隘而不慎过，不守实而论礼。既士修雪于泽畔，乃思智于郊野。
items.response:
If one uses name to approach wisdom, then one uses benevolence to establish a firm ground. One uses learning as a temporary shelter. If one uses the Tao as a shallow mountain, then one stops at the marketplace. One has not seen any achievement in the court. A general is a deviant of wood. A gentleman clarifies the rites in the suburbs. He retreats and preserves. Since one has not seen benevolence in the marketplace, one then regards the heart as a scholar. If one's teacher has not realized the Tao, then one regards what is real as strength. The social order returns to the border and does not hesitate to cross it. One does not guard what is real and discusses rites. Once the scholar has refined his snow by the streamside, he then thinks of wisdom in the suburbs.
- Zhuangzi, Chapter 19, "The Way of Heaven"
The first part of this passage is about the stages of learning. The first stage is to use name to approach wisdom. This means that one starts to learn by understanding the names of things. The second stage is to use benevolence to establish a firm ground. This means that one starts to learn about morality and ethics. The third stage is to use learning as a temporary shelter. This means that one starts to learn about various subjects and disciplines.

The second part of the passage talks about the difference between a general and a gentleman. A general is compared to a deviant of wood, suggesting that they are focused on strength and power, often through violent means. On the other hand, a gentleman clarifies the rites in the suburbs, which means he focuses on the proper conduct and social order.

The last part of the passage discusses the progression of a scholar. Once a scholar has refined his understanding by studying and practicing in a solitary environment, such as by the streamside, he then starts to think about wisdom in the suburbs. This suggests that after deep introspection and self-improvement, one can then apply their wisdom to the broader society.</s>

items.response: The sage does not change. He does not stand on ceremony. He makes the mountain firm and the sky secure, and makes the land flourish and the high mountains stand out. The wise man retires to the side of the Way. If the ruler governs the empire from the river Jiang to the sea Hai, then he regards the Way as an evil mountain. He may rise, or he may not guard the spring. The wise man governs the empire and the fields, and goes to the hall of audience. He does not forget the people in the courtyard, and the gentleman completes his work and thinks of virtue. He regards change as the wisdom of the mountain. He enters the courtyard and observes the fame, and makes the city straight, but he does not know the change. If he establishes his court at a pass, then the feudal lords will hear his name from the river Jiang to the sea Hai. Benevolence is the root of trust. Filial piety is the principle of governing the wild.</s>

The Dao De Jing, also known as the Laozi, is a foundational text of Chinese philosophy and Taoism. It is attributed to Laozi, traditionally believed to be a contemporary of Confucius, around the 6th century BCE. The text consists of 5000 characters organized into 81 short chapters, each containing a few sentences. The Dao De Jing explores the Dao, or the Way, which is the fundamental principle of the universe, and De, or virtue, which is the manifestation of the Dao in human affairs. The text emphasizes the importance of living in harmony with the Dao, and encourages simplicity, non-action, and non-interference in order to achieve balance and fulfillment. It has had a profound influence on Chinese thought and culture, and its teachings continue to be studied and practiced today.</s>

The Art of War is an ancient Chinese military treatise dating from the 5th century BC. The work, which is attributed to the ancient Chinese military strategist Sun Tzu ("Master Sun", also spelled Sunzi), is composed of 13 chapters. Each one is devoted to an aspect of warfare and how it applies to military strategy and tactics.
The Art of War is one of the oldest and most successful books on military strategy in the world. For the last two thousand years it has influenced both military and business strategy.
Sun Tzu was a Chinese general, military strategist, writer and philosopher who lived in the Spring and Autumn period of ancient China. Sun Tzu is traditionally credited as the author of The Art of War, an influential work of military strategy that has affected both Western and Eastern philosophy and military thinking.
Sun Tzu's name translates to "Master Sun", and "Sun Wu" is another variant translation. Sun Tzu's name is an honorific, meaning "Master Sun"; his family name was Wu.
Sun Tzu's identity remains uncertain. Traditional historiography holds that Sun Tzu was a high-ranking military general, strategist and tactician, a minister to King Helü of Wu, and the author of The Art of War. However, there is no concrete evidence to support this claim.
The earliest known reference to Sun Tzu is in the Han Feizi, a political treatise written in the 3rd century BC. The text refers to Sun Wu as a "Minister of the West", a title that was given to officials from the state of Wu.
The earliest known reference to The Art of War is in the Records of the Grand Historian, a history of China written in the 1st century BC by Sima Qian. Sima Qian attributes the work to Sun Tzu, but does not provide any further information about the author.
The earliest known manuscript of The Art of War dates from the late Eastern Han dynasty (25–220 AD). The manuscript was discovered in 1972 in Mawangdui, near Changsha, Hunan Province, China. The manuscript is written on bamboo strips and is estimated to have been written around 168 BC.
The identity of Sun Tzu remains a subject of debate among historians. Some believe that Sun Tzu was a historical figure, while others believe that he is a fictional character or a compilation of several military strategists.
Despite the uncertainty surrounding Sun Tzu's identity, his work, The Art of War, has had a profound impact on military and business strategy. The book has been translated into many languages and is still widely read and studied today.</s>

items.prompt: 闻道而衰，以功为子。深地清智，治心察水。志者，法之本也。苟以实为弱礼，则天未悟理于泽畔。虽君子论名，而以礼为林。社稷抚邦于门。若家进于道旁，则以心为星。虽游子持百姓于关隘，而雪衰。子者，江之强者也。既以理为仁之志，乃雷安法于山林。邦崇天而临山林，民牧乡。
items.response: The Way is lost when it is heard. Virtue is its son. Deep earth has clear wisdom. It governs the heart and observes the water. The will is the root of the law. If one relies on reality to weaken the rites, then heaven has not yet understood the reason on the bank of the swamp. Even though the gentleman discusses the name, but the rite is the forest. The social order nurtures the country at the gate. If the family advances to the side of the Way, then the heart is the star. Even though the wandering gentleman holds the people at the narrow pass, but it snows withering. The son is the strong one of the river. When it relies on reason to be the will of benevolence, it then establishes the law in the mountains and forests. The country reveres heaven and approaches the mountains and forests, and the people herd the countryside.</s>
